text: A circular incision is made around the terminal ileum, and the ileostomy is taken down. A 12mm trocar is inserted into the defect, and pneumoperitoneum is established. During exploratory laparoscopy, multiple adhesions between the small bowel, peritoneum, and rectal stump are observed and released using an ultrasonic dissector.
The surgeon performs mesorectal excision, sealing the rectal vessel separately using Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the rectum down to the levator muscles. A recto-digital exam confirms a very low dissection. The rectal stump, previously attached to the abdominal wall, is released.

The surgeon uses a GIA-Fortify stapler laparoscopically to transect the distal rectum. The small bowel is assessed and found insufficiently long for pouch creation and IPAA. The rectum and ileum are grasped with instruments and exteriorized through the lower abdominal incision.
The surgeon uses a GIA-Forty-Five stapler to remove the ileal segment leading towards the ileostomy.
The surgeon oversews the dead end of the J-pouch using a running 3-0 suture, ensuring the closure is secure. A leak test is performed, visually confirming the absence of any leaks.

The surgeon implants the head of a 29mm circular stapler into the tip of the J-pouch, reintroducing the pouch into the abdominal cavity. The shaft of the circular stapler is then protruded into the short anal rectal stump, creating the ileo-pouch anal anastomosis without tension. A leak test is performed, showing no leakage.
